title: New Study Shows Sweeteners Make You Hungrier

chunks.title: Introduction to Pure Protein Bars
chunks.summary: The video begins by discussing various protein bars, notably a 'galactic brownie' flavor marketed as healthy due to its high protein content. The hosts critique the marketing and ingredient list, emphasizing that these products can be misleading despite appearing healthy.
chunks.transcript: What about this one here? Pure protein bars. Now, this one, it kind of the marketing makes it look healthy, but it says 20 gram of protein on it. And I see the picture here. It's a galactic brownie, which sounds delicious. Man, I bet this does taste really good. My mouth is actually watering a little bit. It says naturally and artificially flavored. Oh, it's gluten-free, so of course it's healthy. The gravel in my driveway is also gluten-free. So, it's worth remembering that. My eyes are bad. So, I'll have you look at that in the ingredients here. Holy moly, what is going on? There's more ingredients there. If we wanted to read all the ingredients, it would take two or three hours. I feel like >> there's got to be at least 30

chunks.title: Concerns About Ingredients
chunks.summary: The hosts dive deeper into the ingredient list of the protein bar, pointing out harmful additives like titanium dioxide and artificial sweeteners like sucralose. They discuss the potential effects of these ingredients on gut health and hunger levels.
chunks.transcript: ingredients here. Um, and I wonder I don't know if the FDA has any font size requirements on the ingredients. I mean, who could read that? >> Yeah. Yeah. I can't. It just looks like a blur. >> Look at the size of it. It's so small. >> Well, what's funny is the first word I see is pure, >> right? pure protein. So, this is these are interesting because they're part of a growing section of the grocery store, which is sort of the protein enriched foods that masquerade as healthy, which are not actually healthy. And you can see that if you do a little more digging beyond the calories, because I mean, look at to our previous discussion of calories. Big bold letters, 180 calories, doesn't sound like that many calories to me >> and it's got 20 grams of protein. That is healthier, right? That is essentially where most of the consumers may stop. And that's that's how they market it on the front. 20 gram of protein, 1 gram of sugar, >> and 180 calories. These must be healthy. But we begin to read the ingredients and we see things like, well, titanium dioxide. Um, that's not a great thing for humans. You don't want to be eating titanium dioxide. Probably bad for your gut. And the last couple of ingredients are the food dyes, which have been recently regulated against. So you got yellow five, red three, yellow six, and blue one. But it doesn't stop there. So, there are a couple problems with sucralose. There was recently a study where they actually gave people a similar amount of sucralose to sugar versus water in drinks. And even more than sugar, sucralose made people hungrier

chunks.title: Importance of Protein in Diet
chunks.summary: Transitioning to a broader discussion, the hosts emphasize the importance of protein for satiety and discuss the concept of protein leverage. They suggest a practical guideline of consuming one gram of protein per pound of goal body weight to meet daily protein needs.
chunks.transcript: afterward. I think that for most of us getting enough protein is important. There is something called this protein leverage hypothesis which is not the only thing that affects our satiety, the amount of protein we get in a day. But I think most of us can have more satiety that is less hunger if we get enough protein per day. Now if you ask 10 nutrition experts you might get seven answers about how much protein someone needs. I think that a good rule of thumb for people which is probably even a little of an overestimate is one gram of protein per pound of goal body weight. Right? So, I'm about 160 lbs, which means I'm aiming for 160 gram of protein per day. I probably don't need quite that much, maybe eight grams, but it's just easier to remember one gram. And that's goal body weight. Now, when you think about that, it's how does that translate, right? So, you think a pound of meat, one of the single ingredient foods around the outside of the grocery store, a pound of meat has around 100 grams of protein. So, the other thing that I eat with protein is milk. So that has maybe six grams of protein per serving for me. So I'm I'm going to have to think like, okay, if I really want to meet my protein needs for the day, I need to be thinking, how am I going to get that much meat? And meat is good for humans and has lots of micronutrients which help with satiety long term. I

chunks.title: Quality of Protein Sources
chunks.summary: The narrative shifts to the importance of sourcing protein from whole, high-quality foods rather than processed items. The hosts recommend cooking meats and fish and advocate for consuming protein at multiple meals throughout the day for optimal health.
chunks.transcript: think a major issue is people are not getting enough protein. So I see where they're coming from here. I mean, it's pure protein, right? But I don't think that you want to get protein at the expense of artificial sweeteners, sucralose, food dyes, um kagenan is also in here which is harmful for the gut. You want to get protein from foods that are um either convenient and higher quality or foods that you can take the time to prepare. So this is where it comes to you didn't take the time to prepare. So cooking some meat or fish or chicken for yourself throughout the day. And I think most of us eating protein at two or three meals a day is going to be helpful for long-term satiety, lack of

chunks.title: Healthy Snacking Options
chunks.summary: The hosts discuss alternatives to protein bars for quick snacks, suggesting single-ingredient options like fruit or cheese for better nutrition. They emphasize the benefits of simplicity and convenience in food choices while shopping.
chunks.transcript: hunger, weight loss, and resilience. Right? We know that when we get enough protein, our bones are stronger, our ligaments are stronger. Those are the things that connect the bones to each other. Our skin is better. That's how we make collagen is from protein, especially bioavailable animal protein. There are more and more of these these items in the grocery store that are simple ingredients that are convenient, but we need to expand that category because most of it is this health health, you know, advertisement, which is really not that healthy, right? It's it's, you know, messaging forward foods that are not actually really that healthy for us. >> And I think when we're when we're in a position where we're going to buy one of these bars, it's often because we're hungry, not because we're we're thinking to ourselves, okay, let's make sure I get the right amount of protein today. Right? If I'm just focused on protein, I might be able to lean into the prep. But it's usually hunger that drives us to do this. And we say, we look at the candy bar aisle and we think, well, this is probably better than, you know, a Kit Kat bar or something like that.
